caveats: a: The occupation mix includes non-drilling support services.
a: Task exposure is inferred rather than observed labor substitution.
rho: No direct survey measures five-year implementation in LMM drillers.
rho: Safety and customer-control requirements may slow deployment more than technical capability suggests.
e: The injected n is margin-bridged from size classes using a sector EBITDA margin and may be distorted by the drilling cycle.
e: Rig ownership and operating entity can differ.
s5: Owner age is not an observed deal probability.
s5: Asset transactions are common and must not be counted as firm control transfers.
q: Contract terms vary by basin, rig class, utilization, and customer.
q: The source is a large public contractor and may not reflect LMM bargaining power.
d5: Energy outlooks are highly price- and policy-sensitive.
d5: Rig counts do not capture footage, lateral length, or service intensity.
d5: The 2030 outlook covers all US production, not LMM contractors.
o: Operator consolidation and self-performance can reduce the independent share even though physical drilling persists.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring outsourced rig-and-crew service sold primarily through daywork/dayrate, well-based, or performance-based contracts, with mobilization and lower rates for some non-operating periods; activity and pricing track customer drilling budgets and rig supply.
lens.excluded_activities: E&P owners drilling solely for their own account; captive drilling units; passive rig lessors without operators; completion and ancillary oilfield services classified in 213112; offshore or land contractors outside the EBITDA band; shells and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors repeatedly drilling oil and gas wells for external exploration and production customers on a contract or fee basis, including spudding, drilling, redrilling, and directional drilling performed with contractor-operated rigs and crews.

a.bridge: BLS measures NAICS 213100 support activities for mining, not 213111 alone, and does not measure AI exposure. I assign partial exposure to bid preparation, well-plan review, directional optimization support, scheduling, maintenance planning, safety documentation, reporting, payroll, and procurement, while giving little exposure to rig-floor extraction, maintenance, transport, and well-control tasks. Because drilling is likely more rig-floor-intensive than the broader group, this proxy may overstate exposure.
caveats: The occupation mix includes non-drilling support services.
Task exposure is inferred rather than observed labor substitution.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted six-digit SOC roster for 213111 and task-time studies across land drillers covering tendering, well planning, directional support, maintenance, reporting, logistics, HSE, and rig-floor operations.
a.rationale: In the broader industry, construction and extraction occupations are 46.43% of employment, roustabouts alone are 11.92%, and installation/maintenance occupations are 9.16%. High-wage engineering, supervisory, planning, and administrative work is more exposed, but the physical and safety-critical core is not.

d5.bridge: EIA rig counts and production outlooks measure upstream activity and output, not five-year constant-quality demand for LMM contract drilling. I use rigs as the closest current quantity indicator, adjust downward for continuing drilling-productivity gains that allow output with fewer rigs, and allow oil-price and LNG-driven gas cases. Production can remain flat while drilling-service demand falls, so production forecasts alone would bias the estimate upward.
caveats: Energy outlooks are highly price- and policy-sensitive.
Rig counts do not capture footage, lateral length, or service intensity.
The 2030 outlook covers all US production, not LMM contractors.
d5.change_evidence: Basin-level five-year operator capital budgets, Enverus well permits and spuds, rig tender backlogs, dayrates, attrition/reactivation, drilling days per well, and oil/gas price sensitivities through 2031.
d5.rationale: EIA reports 553 rotary rigs operating in May 2026, close to the prior five months, while Patterson-UTI's US average operating rigs fell from 124 in 2023 to 112 in 2024 and 100 in 2025. July 2026 STEO forecasts US crude production of 13.8 million barrels per day in 2026 and 14.0 in 2027, but AEO 2026 expects sub-$70 real Brent through 2030 and declining US production through the mid-2030s in nearly all cases. Gas and LNG demand support an upside case; efficiency and price weakness drive the downside.

caveats: The injected n is margin-bridged from size classes using a sector EBITDA margin and may be distorted by the drilling cycle.
Rig ownership and operating entity can differ.
e.change_evidence: A current 213111 firm roster matched to active rigs, ultimate ownership, US external revenue, normalized through-cycle EBITDA, customer concentration, and operating versus stacked status.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is already contract or fee drilling for others, so most true operating firms meet the external-service element. Eligibility is reduced by under-scale owner-operators, inactive or stacked-rig entities, subsidiaries and captive units, offshore/global operators, severe customer concentration, and businesses whose normalized EBITDA falls outside the band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 213111 ($5,438,207,663) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 213111 ($20,106,048,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0776 (margins.json[213]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Oilfield Svcs/Equip. EBITDA/Sales 7.76%); revenue/employee $377,387 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 213111); class <5: 907 firms, avg revenue/firm $525,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $40,755 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 161 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,179,137 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $169,101 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 159 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,559,824 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $353,842 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 185 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,453,141 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,043,964 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,626,929 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,773,450 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $333,547,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $25,883,286 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 241

caveats: Operator consolidation and self-performance can reduce the independent share even though physical drilling persists.
o.change_evidence: Observed adoption of remotely operated autonomous rigs with materially reduced onsite contractor responsibility, or sustained E&P insourcing that eliminates independent drilling contractors rather than changing their staffing.
o.rationale: A physical well still requires a responsible contractor to deploy and operate rigs, manage crews, maintain equipment, control pressure, and execute safely. Automation may reduce crew and planning labor, and some E&P customers may insource or consolidate vendors, but software alone cannot deliver the service within five years.

q.bridge: Patterson-UTI's filing describes US drilling contracts and activity for a large public contractor, not LMM firms or AI-benefit pass-through. I infer retention from dayrates, competitive bidding, reduced rates during downtime, and growing performance-based terms: fixed contract periods can preserve gains initially, while renewals and shared performance targets return gains to customers. Large-company bargaining structures may understate retention for differentiated regional specialists but overstate it where small firms lack pricing power.
caveats: Contract terms vary by basin, rig class, utilization, and customer.
The source is a large public contractor and may not reflect LMM bargaining power.
q.change_evidence: Representative LMM drilling contracts showing term, cost escalation, dayrate adjustment, performance bonuses/penalties, open-book provisions, rebid cadence, downtime treatment, and post-automation margin bridges.
q.rationale: Daywork revenue is paid per utilized day, with lower rates during moves or interruptions, and performance contracts share outcomes around agreed targets. Efficiency that reduces internal administrative cost can be retained during a contract; faster well delivery or reduced downtime may be shared explicitly or repriced through competitive tenders.

caveats: No direct survey measures five-year implementation in LMM drillers.
Safety and customer-control requirements may slow deployment more than technical capability suggests.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployment evidence from independent drillers showing adoption, labor hours, nonproductive time, maintenance failures, safety incidents, well-plan deviations, and integration cost over multiple customer programs.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation is plausible in commercial, scheduling, reporting, preventive-maintenance, and decision-support workflows. Remote sites, inconsistent data, legacy rig controls, customer approval, cyber risk, union/change constraints, and catastrophic well-control consequences require human validation and slow autonomous deployment.

s5.bridge: Census ABS owner-age tables cover employer businesses generally, not eligible drilling contractors or control transfers. I use owner aging only as succession pressure, then discount for corporate ownership, family/internal succession, asset sales without operating-company control, bankruptcies, closures, and transactions delayed by the commodity cycle; broad owner aging likely overstates completed transfers here.
caveats: Owner age is not an observed deal probability.
Asset transactions are common and must not be counted as firm control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of verified PitchBook, Enverus, GF Data, bankruptcy, and rig-asset transactions matched to eligible firms and tagged for whole-company control versus asset-only sales.
s5.rationale: Capital intensity and cyclicality drive consolidation and distressed asset trades, but those are not always qualifying transfers of durable operations. A buyer must underwrite rigs, contracts, crews, liabilities, and customer concentration, limiting executable control changes.

principal_driver: High labor intensity and recurring outsourced rig operations create a real but bounded workflow-automation opportunity.
principal_weakness: Commodity-driven utilization and productivity gains can reduce drilling demand even while US hydrocarbon production remains high.

sources.supports: Defines establishments drilling oil and gas wells for others on a contract or fee basis.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 213111 Drilling Oil and Gas Wells

sources.supports: Broader occupation mix: construction/extraction 46.43%, roustabouts 11.92%, and installation/maintenance/repair 9.16% of employment.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Support Activities for Mining

sources.supports: Describes drilling, casing, maintenance, and well-control work and states trained personnel are essential for well control.
sources.title: OSHA Oil and Gas Well Drilling eTool

sources.supports: Details pressure monitoring, drilling-fluid control, BOP installation/testing, and trained-personnel requirements.
sources.title: OSHA Drilling Well Control

sources.supports: Monthly US rotary rigs averaged 553 in May 2026: 535 onshore, 18 offshore, 417 oil-directed, and 127 gas-directed.
sources.title: EIA Crude Oil and Natural Gas Drilling Activity

sources.supports: Most cases keep real Brent below $70 through 2030 and show US crude production declining through the mid-2030s; 2025 production was 13.6 million barrels per day.
sources.title: Annual Energy Outlook 2026: Oil and natural gas

sources.supports: Describes daywork and performance-based contracts; reports US average operating rigs of 124 in 2023, 112 in 2024, and 100 in 2025 and wells drilled declining from 2,530 to 2,090.
sources.title: Patterson-UTI Energy 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Official Census Age of Owner tables for employer businesses, used only as a broad succession proxy.
sources.title: ABS Characteristics of Business Owners: 2024 Tables

sources.supports: Forecast US crude production of 13.8 million barrels per day in 2026 and 14.0 in 2027, with LNG exports increasing from 17 to 19 Bcf/d.
sources.title: EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook, July 2026

title: Drilling Oil and Gas Wells
